data.title: The Market Forgot Nvidia: Big Mistake
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), once the undisputed king of AI, seems to have been forgotten by investors.

Interest has moved beyond GPUs and into the new AI bottlenecks like memory. And even within GPUs, Nvidia’s dominance is being challenged.

But does that justify the current price action in Nvidia?

The stock has been flat in the last six months, while earnings keep expanding and AI demand continues to heat up.

Nvidia is now the cheapest it has ever been in this AI cycle, if you ask me, and this sets it up as an outperformer in the coming months.

Nvidia Stock’s Stagnation

The stock has been pretty much flat for over six months now. Historically, we've seen this happen before, and it's usually been followed by a rally.

It happened back in 2024 and also in 2021. It’s understandable. Sometimes expectations get ahead of themselves, and both fundamentals and technicals have to reset.

But we’ve waited long enough.

I highlighted how Nvidia is now a critical strategic asset in my last article, and why it deserves a higher valuation.

Today, we highlight this further with some clear evidence from the GPU market.

Valuation Metrics

Let’s begin by stating the obvious. Nvidia is now historically undervalued.

The stock is trading well below its 5 YR average on most valuation metrics. Forward P/E of 21,46 is very compelling, and of course, both the PEG and forward PEG.

This makes no sense, especially when the rest of the AI market is very much chugging along.

The AI Divergence

While Nvidia has stagnated, all other areas of the AI market are lighting up.

Memory stocks, like Micron (MU) have exploded higher, and fiber optics stocks like Lumentum (LITE) have also seen a great repricing.

Even within the more strictly related semiconductor stocks, NVDA is a laggard. Both Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Broadcom (AVGO) have outperformed Nvidia.

Why The NVDA Discount Makes No Sense

You might think that these lesser-known AI stocks have just been playing catch-up in terms of valuation, but that’s a line we crossed a while ago.

Nvidia is cheaper than most of the stocks listed above, with the exception of Micron, which I am also very bullish on.

You could argue that while memory and connectivity are now bottlenecks, the market is now well supplied with GPUs.

But that’s not what price is signaling.

As we can see from this chart, GPU prices have moved higher and higher in the last 5 years, while Nvidia’s P/E has continued to fall.

While perhaps GPU prices did stagnate in 2025, we are beginning to see GPU rental prices pick up on 2026, signaling increasing demand.

The rental price for an H200, for example, has more than doubled in price since the beginning of the year.

This bodes very well for Nvidia, and points to a divergence that I cannot understand.

Price Target

Nvidia is still growing at a healthy rate, and there’s even potential for further margin expansion.

Plus, this is still the company that saw AI coming years in advance, and that should count for something.

Nvidia is projected to reach around $8.30 EPS by year-end, up over 73% from last year.

Even applying a modest P/E of 25, that would put Nvidia in the $222 price range, roughly a 23% increase from today’s price, and likely in the low range.

More likely, Nvidia will beat expectations as AI heats up, and a shift in sentiment could push the P/E closer to previous levels of around 30.

At 30 P/E and EPS closer to 9, we’re looking at a price range of closer to $270.

This lines up better with my technical analysis, in which I have used a three-point fib extension to project some key fib levels. The 1.618 gets us just shy of $300, an important psychological threshold, too.

Nvidia could arguably be overvalued at that point, but once animal spirits take over, and if the AI narrative picks up more steam, this is within reach.

The Issue With Nvidia

The problem a lot of people have with Nvidia, perhaps, is that it’s already too big.

It controls over 90% of the GPU market, and some hyperscalers are deliberately trying to diversify away from Nvidia in order to not to be locked in.

While there’s some merit to this, and it’s the reason I have been more bullish on AMD in the last year, this narrative is already priced in, and Nvidia has, in fact, done a lot to address this issue.

For example, Nvidia has released a lot of open-source models. making it easy for everyone to participate in the ecosystem.

On the other hand, yes, Nvidia is trying to sell the whole AI factory. The issue here is whether the whole Nvidia ecosystem is compelling enough for its clients to go all in on it.

If market share and performance is any indication, then Nvidia still has a commanding lead and will continue to grow in line with the AI market.

Final Thoughts

The market has rightly, in my opinion, been chasing the AI narrative. But it has left Nvidia behind.

We now have a compelling entry point, and I believe Nvidia could begin to outperform, especially as other AI names that have run too far begin to deflate, and Nvidia continues to prove to the market why it’s still the king of AI.

data.title: 3 Reasons Why I Remain Bullish On SCHB, And 2 To Sell It Immediately
data.description:
When I think about Schwab U.S. Broad Market ETF (SCHB), I think of a benchmark in the US large-cap market. And when I think about the US large-cap market, today I think … “What a mess!”

Yes, because there are 3 big reasons to be bullish, 2 fundamental and 1 technical. But also 2 great reasons to stay away from it.

In my opinion, if it weren’t for the last of these, I would have given a BUY rating. For now I remain on HOLD. Before explaining why …

What is SCHB

It is a passive ETF designed as “core” exposure to the US equity market belonging to the Large Blend, broad category. The stated benchmark is the Dow Jones U.S. Broad Stock Market Index, which includes about 2,500 listed U.S. companies. The stated objective is to replicate it as closely as possible. Structural implication? The strategy aims for market beta, not alpha;

What has made it widely used is its cost: 0.03%, an extremely competitive cost and practically in line with the main broad market/large-cap peers, fully offset by a 30-day SEC Yield of 1.15%. Not surprisingly, today it has an AUM above $39 billion.

Composition

The index it tracks uses a float-adjusted market capitalization weighted method, with sampling techniques, which produces a “winner-takes-all” type dynamic, and like other indices, the fund tends to reflect the leadership of mega caps and to participate mainly in rallies driven by Big Tech and growth. Here the top ten account for over 30%, and strong exposures can be seen to stocks such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (and the other Mag 7).

Peers

In this sense, the comparison with ETFs that have the S&P 500 as a benchmark, such as the famous and widely used SPY, can be interesting. This is a tabular reworking of SA data that can help.

In essence, there are no major differences except that SPY is slightly more tech-heavy. This also means fairly similar valuations; trying to weight by % of sector allocation, this is, in my opinion, a good comparison in valuation terms:

Which brings the final analysis directly back to performance, which naturally slightly favors SPY since 2022 due to the boom in the tech segment.

Despite this, I prefer SCHB for a very simple reason: a lower expense ratio (SPY has an expense ratio of 0.09%), compared to SCHB’s 2397 holdings versus SPY’s 504.

Does It Make Sense Today?

Considering SCHB as a potential core solution to cover the US component of a portfolio, the question I ask myself today is, given these valuations, does it make sense to take a position today from a forward-looking perspective? My opinion: “It depends.”

  1. Good forward valuations: The forward valuations of the most sensitive component of SCHB (info tech) have now returned close to the average. In theory, with valuations at 20.3x, we are at almost the 50th percentile of the 10-year distribution and above the 74th of the 30-year one.
  2. Rates already high: Powell over the last three years has not made the mistake of cutting interest rates below the neutral level, and the fed funds are already at 3.5-3.75%. According to the CME FedWatch tool, the probabilities of a hike today are at 2.6% (despite inflation expectations having risen). In fact, the 1Y break-even inflation has exceeded 4.5%, but the 5- and 10-year ones remain within the pre-energy shock threshold of March. If energy inflation were actually only temporarily contagious, and the conflict were to end in the short term as suggested by the (provocative) communications of the White House, this “flash war” would have generated a discount relative to expected valuations that is attractive.
  3. Technical evidence: I am not a fan of using technical analysis unless the fundamental analysis has already given me elements to reason on. And I do not use many indicators, only 1: RSI on a weekly timeframe set between 30 and 70 points. Nothing exceptional. But extraordinary, in my opinion, for those like me who have long horizons. And the reasoning is simple. Since 2012, the RSI lows (30 points) have been tactical zones for those working with DCA.

Risk

So far, it seems like a rare opportunity. But if we introduce these two risks, we immediately realize that the “discount” is not exactly free.

  • CapEx does not lead to a sufficient increase in margins to justify these earnings expectations. If EPS fell, then forward valuations were not convenient; in fact, it was a trap. And a clue in this sense is seen when, instead of forward P/E, we use forward P/FCF as a metric. The “discount” (if we can call it that) contract (in fact, disappears).

  • The conflict in Iran, even without worsening costs for companies (higher interest rates), can impact revenues (contraction in consumption), still reducing EPS. This would make valuations unsustainable.

My Opinion

So it is true that forward valuations have returned to the average, but are the historical valuations (10 and 30 years) of the tech segment consistent with the characteristics of today’s sector? Because if CAPEX increases, tech companies become capital-intensive assets, like industrials. And those trade at very different forward valuations, lower.

On this point, it should be considered that big tech, from one year to another, has increased CapEx by +70%. And it is the same reason why the expected EPS of the tech sector is so high (above 30% for 2026). Therefore, looking at P/FCF today, in my opinion, is wrong if one wants to have a forward-looking view over the next 5/10 years.

What really worries the market, in my opinion, is the second point: the idea that inflation could contract consumption, not only in the tech segment, although it is mainly the one driving the declines of recent days. But in this sense, two of the 3 reasons listed, in my opinion, precisely mitigate this risk.

For this reason, I think the right rating is HOLD: valuations are attractive, even net of P/FCF (in my view temporarily high), but the economic impact will mainly depend on the evolution of the energy context, therefore indirectly on geopolitical reasons (outside the forward control of the investor).

Conclusion

To conclude, valuations compress if we look at forward valuations. I do not think that the lack of FCF can be a problem. The fact that the economic impact is difficult to estimate makes it hard to say whether these valuations are realistically at a discount. But in relative terms, today they are, also from a technical point of view. For this reason, I think a SELL rating is not justified at all.

data.title: Rivian: A Platform-Driven Growth Story (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Intro

I recently did an analysis of First Solar (FSLR), which I came across while looking for potential beneficiaries of the current high energy prices. Although my analysis found First Solar to be an impressive, fully American solar‑module producer and an attractive investment, it still isn’t the outright winner in this high‑price setup.

The reasoning behind this conclusion is that U.S. natural‑gas prices haven’t risen in line with global prices due to the U.S.’s limited gas export capacity. Since natural gas is the most important source of electricity generation in the U.S., and First Solar sells utility‑scale modules to utilities, it follows naturally that there is no additional demand boost coming from higher electricity‑production costs at the moment.

As I kept exploring the so-called alternative‑energy space more broadly, I noticed Rivian on my coverage list. Since I haven’t updated it since rating it a Hold back in November last year — a good call, as the stock is trading at nearly the same level, around $15— it felt like a appropriate time to revisit it, especially to see how it could shine as a strong alternative to ICE (internal combustion engine) vehicles in the current volatile environment. In my view, relatively stable electricity prices — supported by muted natural‑gas prices and contrasted with the sharp jump at gas stations — could create a favorable setup for electric vehicles to attract increased attention.

Jumping ahead a bit, I found a Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN) case similar to First Solar — the technology story at play here is far more compelling than any potential (short term?) benefit from the spike in oil prices. Rivian increasingly looks like an emerging, attractive technology‑platform play rather than just an EV maker.

Rivian's Uniqueness

If we set Tesla (TSLA) aside — which I think operates in a different league with a scale that isn’t really comparable yet — there really aren’t many pure‑play U.S. electric‑vehicle producers left. Rivian and Lucid (LCID) basically make up the list. You could even call them brave survivors, considering how many other contenders have sought bankruptcy protection in recent years.

Building a car company virtually from scratch, armed mostly with a strong vision, was long seen as almost impossible, given the complexity and capital intensity of the auto industry — until Tesla proved otherwise about a decade ago. That said, Tesla’s story attracted a wave of new bold entrants, but the reality is that only two with meaningful scale have remained viable.

If we take a closer look at these two great survivors — Rivian and Lucid — they seem quite different in terms of market‑segment focus. Rivian’s brand seemingly targets a younger and more adventurous audience, while Lucid is clearly focused on the luxury high‑end segment at the moment. And while Rivian’s current models, the R1T and R1S, are still roughly $100,000‑class vehicles like Lucid’s current offerings, Rivian's upcoming R2 and R3 models are set to start at around $45,000 and $35,000, respectively, aimed to attract a wider mass market audience.

In addition, it’s becoming increasingly evident that Rivian’s multi‑year collaboration with Volkswagen (VLKAF) is starting to pay off and is giving Rivian meaningful new revenue opportunities ahead. The latest announcement — that yet another milestone has been reached, this time a VW ID.Every1 vehicle running jointly developed software successfully passing key winter tests — triggered the release of the next $1 billion funding tranche to Rivian under the Rivian-Volkswagen agreement announced in 2024.

What Makes the VW Collaboration a Big Deal

Given these latest developments, Rivian could look like it’s moving in roughly the same direction as Tesla — even if it’s far behind in scale. They’re doing everything in‑house, from all the software — including robotaxi and full self‑driving ("FSD") — to car manufacturing, and even their own chip development.

But here’s the key difference. While Tesla keeps its ecosystem strictly closed, Rivian seems to bet on offering its platform — together with its AV and robotaxi tech — to other carmakers. That’s a very different strategy. And if Rivian can prove that its approach works, it opens a potentially huge market for them. Early progress seems really promising, according to Rivian’s CEO Robert Scaringe in the latest earnings call:

...the first 13 months of establishing a joint venture with Volkswagen, we've testing on multiple VW Group products. And I think the true demonstration and existence proof, if you will, of the scalability of our technology in terms of being able to work across multiple form factors, different price points, different brands and importantly, be productized into a platform that can go across a large existing OEM.

What makes this favorable progress even more significant for Rivian, in my view, is the fact that Volkswagen put a significant effort costing them nearly $20 billion over the years to build its own internal software unit, called Cariad, and only changed its strategy last November. Cariad’s new strategy shifts away from developing software in-house and instead focuses on bringing in external software to integrate across the group’s brands. The latest news, alongside Rivian’s successful developments, includes VW strengthening its collaboration with Qualcomm (QCOM), as well as deepening its ties with Xpeng (XPEV) in the Chinese market.

So, we can conclude that — let’s say still small — American EV maker has secured increasingly strong backing from one of the world’s largest automakers. Furthermore, VW has essentially shut down its own internal software development efforts. To me, it highlights the remarkable scalability potential of Rivian's software platform, and that’s a pretty big deal.

And yet another major global player sees real potential in Rivian’s recent progress — Uber (UBER) is putting real money behind it.

Uber Steps In With Another Strong Signal

The latest announcement about the Rivian–Uber collaboration adds, in my view, yet another strong pillar supporting Rivian’s bullish narrative. Fully autonomous vehicles are the prime target for vehicle‑software developers, and Uber needs — one could even say desperately — to secure its global leadership in ridesharing by partnering with potential winning AV/robotaxi developers. Yes, Uber collaborates with almost every major AV player out there to make sure it doesn't miss future winners, but each partnership differs in depth. The latest commitment agreed with Rivian is on a different level and outshines Uber’s previous ones in terms of deal value. The $1.25 billion total package includes an initial $300 million equity investment, expected to follow shortly after signing, along with an agreement to purchase up to 50,000 AV‑ready vehicles if Rivian meets specific autonomous‑driving milestones by set deadlines.

Dara Khosrowshahi, CEO of Uber said:

“We’re big believers in Rivian’s approach—designing the vehicle, compute platform, and software stack together, while maintaining end-to-end control of scaled manufacturing and supply in the U.S. That vertical integration, combined with data from their growing consumer vehicle base and experience managing the complexities of commercial fleets, gives us conviction to set these ambitious but achievable targets.”

Latest Developments

Q1 Deliveries

Rivian produced 10,236 vehicles in Q1 and delivered 10,365 vehicles, up some 20% YoY. The production and delivery results for the quarter were roughly in line with the company’s and analysts' outlook. In the same quarter last year, Rivian produced 14,611 vehicles and delivered 8,640. The company also reiterated its commitment to the 2026 delivery guidance, expecting to deliver between 62,000 and 67,000 vehicles.

The strong delivery figures are particularly noteworthy considering the expiration of the $7,500 tax credits in September of last year. Furthermore, according to the company, Rivian has sold approximately 6,000 more vehicles than it produced over the past twelve months, resulting in a significant reduction in inventory ahead of the R2 launch.

Q4 FY2025 Earnings and Outlook 2026

The $839 million of Q4 automotive revenues, compared to $1,520 million in the same quarter in 2024, saw a significant 45 percent decrease YoY. This was mainly due to a $270 million drop in regulatory credit sales, fewer vehicle deliveries after tax credits expired, and a lower average sales price.

Software and services revenue came in at $447 million, up from $214 million YoY — a remarkable 109% increase YoY. This growth was mainly driven by higher demand for vehicle electrical architecture and software development services from the joint venture with Volkswagen, along with increased sales from vehicle trade-ins and repair and maintenance services.

Despite the significant decline in automotive results, markets reacted positively as results overall slightly beat expectations. The real driver, however, was the upbeat 2026 vehicle delivery forecast of 62,000 to 67,000 units, which sparked a rally that pushed Rivian’s shares up more than 20% the next trading day.

Aside from the optimistic delivery estimates, the outlook was quite limited and lacked much detailed information:

CFO comments around deliveries and upcoming R2 launch:

"We expect total deliveries of approximately 9,000 to 11,000 per quarter in the first half of 2026. We plan to start production of the R2 launch variant with a single shift and expect to add a second shift towards the end of the year."

Software and services brought in about $1.6 billion out of Rivian’s $5.4 billion total revenue in 2025. Following an impressive 109% growth in Q4, the CFO highlighted on the conference call that this segment is expected to see around 60% annualized growth in 2026.

Funding for 2026

Rivian ended 2025 with $6.1 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and short‑term investments, and expects to receive roughly $2 billion in additional capital from Volkswagen in 2026, including a $1 billion equity investment in Rivian and another $1 billion in the form of a loan to their software joint venture, RV Tech. Together, the cash on hand and VW infusion could keep the company funded through 2027.

Valuation

Valuing Rivian is a really tough task, in my opinion. What makes it increasingly difficult is the fact that the company hasn’t outlined at what scale financial breakeven might be expected.

I looked around for some real-world examples and found that Tesla reported its first annual profit in 2020, and it needed to produce around 500,000 vehicles and generate roughly $30 billion in revenue to achieve it. And that revenue included regulatory credits and incremental FSD software revenue.

Still, from a free cash flow standpoint, Tesla posted a positive result already in 2019, even without counting the regulatory credits. It sold about 370,000 vehicles that year.

In the best-case scenario, Rivian could reach its full annual production capacity of 215,000 units at its Normal, Illinois plant sometime between 2027 and 2028, though that doesn’t necessarily mean they’ll actually be able to produce at maximum capacity. Automotive manufacturing, as we know, is extraordinarily complex, and a wide range of challenges can arise along the way. And even if production ramps up smoothly, the vehicles still need to be sold, which is getting increasingly difficult in a highly competitive EV market.

Up to an additional 400,000 units of capacity are expected to come online at the Georgia plant in several phases, with the first vehicles projected to roll off the assembly line by late 2028.

In my view, these estimates suggest that profitability based on vehicle production and sales alone is still many years away — potentially not achievable until around 2029–2030. And that’s before considering skeptics who doubt whether Rivian can reach the needed vehicle sales volumes with its current model lineup at all.

What seems clear is that from a purely automotive manufacturing standpoint, Rivian will need to continuously raise additional capital to fund capex and operations in the coming years, meaning that notable dilution for current shareholders is highly likely until the company reaches breakeven.

Dilution?

So let’s look at Rivian’s potential funding needs. As management has stated — and as the actual numbers also show — the company is largely funded through 2027. The projected cash outflow in 2026 is around $4 billion, driven by an estimated EBITDA loss of roughly $2 billion and a similar level of capex.

2027 will very likely show improvement as R2 sales gain traction, but we simply don’t know by how much. Let’s assume Rivian manages to cut its cash burn by half, bringing it down to about $2 billion. Under that scenario, the company would still need to start looking for fresh capital, as liquidity would otherwise dry up, with perhaps $3–4 billion available at the beginning of 2027. CFOs typically avoid waiting until the very last moment in situations like this.

By 2027, however, we should have much better clarity around the most crucial factors shaping Rivian’s path forward: how well the R2 is selling, how effectively Volkswagen has implemented the JV software across its various models, and — most importantly — whether Rivian’s AV and robotaxi initiatives are showing measurable progress. I think these dynamics will determine the funding conditions at that point. In the best‑case scenario, if the market strongly buys into Rivian’s robotaxi and platform potential, a higher stock price could make capital raising significantly easier. It’s worth remembering that Tesla had no difficulty raising equity when market sentiment was firmly behind its emerging growth story.

All that said, in my view it’s impossible — and even makes no sense — to try to calculate any concrete figures today without knowing how these key variables play out. The reality is that profitability based purely on automotive operations is still years away and will almost certainly require additional funding, with meaningful dilution for current shareholders.

To justify Rivian’s current valuation, we need to believe in the company’s ability to continue building a leading software platform, along with genuinely functional AV and robotaxi capabilities. If they succeed — and if the platform proves scalable, with encouraging signs pointing that way so far— the addressable market becomes so large that detailed calculations today are almost irrelevant. The only reasonable conclusion in my view is that the current market cap of around $18 billion clearly does not reflect that potential.

While everyone agrees that the addressable global robotaxi market is enormous, accurate estimates are understandably hard to make. In most cases, the figures are highlighted as being in the trillions. So even with the chance that Rivian could capture 10% of, say, a $1 trillion market, that would already amount to $100 billion.

Final Conclusion on the Rating

To wrap up my valuation perspective, I rely primarily on the following qualitative factors:

  • Both Uber and Volkswagen have shown confidence in Rivian and its platform with significant financial backing. Their latest capital infusions converted to equity at around the current $15 stock price. While their moves may be driven primarily by strategic considerations, they can still be interpreted as signaling confidence in the value implied by Rivian’s current stock price.
  • Volkswagen has successfully tested jointly developed software on its vehicles. Furthermore, they ended their internal software development and have focused solely on external collaborations. I think this adds another layer to the strength of their partnership with Rivian. To put Volkswagen Group’s scale in perspective, it sold nearly 9 million vehicles worldwide in 2025, with roughly 10% of them being EVs. The potential for revenue growth is enormous.
  • According to management, Amazon’s van collaboration, though recently out of the spotlight, is moving along at its own pace. Should Rivian deliver on its AV ambitions, the partnership could create meaningful further upside. Robotaxis get most of the attention today, but commercial freight could become an AV market of at least the same magnitude over time — if not larger.
  • The software platform is designed to be scalable to other OEMs, potentially unlocking another tremendous market opportunity.

Considering all potentially favorable developments, I think Rivian’s current valuation is appealing and presents an attractive risk-reward balance. I now rate Rivian as Buy.

Risks

While the automotive industry as such is inherently complex and carries a wide range of risks that could fill dozens of pages, the key risk in my view right now is the platform Rivian is developing. If the platform succeeds, it could outweigh potential setbacks related to the launch of new models, which always carry heightened execution risk, especially during the first years after launch.

Regarding the much‑discussed and capex‑intensive Georgia plant, where construction has now begun, I believe the risk can be viewed as somewhat diminished given the latest geopolitical headwinds and today’s tariff environment. A next‑generation automotive manufacturing facility built with the latest technologies and located in the U.S. is a valuable asset in its own right once completed.

One could also argue that if Rivian’s upcoming models do not fully utilize the plant’s capacity, the collaboration with Volkswagen could potentially help ensure that any unused capacity still finds productive use.

Takeaway

A great new vehicle is coming, the platform development is making solid progress, and the company has high‑quality backers onboard. Volkswagen, Uber, Amazon and Saudi Abdul Latif Jameel Fund— together owning roughly half of the company — in my view speak for themselves.

I think it’s fair to say that the stars are well aligned for Rivian to create a compelling investment story, potentially even comparable to Tesla’s early days. That said, the next couple of years are crucial, and a lot has to go right for the narrative to gain stronger footing. With the R2 set to start rolling off the assembly line this quarter, we will soon get the first real‑world indication of how things may play out.

I believe the current volatility in the markets offers a good opportunity to gradually start building a position in Rivian. However, given the broader uncertainties in markets and the company‑specific risks highlighted, I think it is more prudent to do so patiently, spreading purchases gradually over time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kura Sushi: Recovering Comps May Signal Beginning Of Turnaround (Upgrade)
data.description:
Amid a very volatile stock market in 2026, the best move for investors is to be very active and vigilant. Valuations and fundamental stories are changing in real time, especially as a choppy macroeconomy impacts weaker companies more harshly. Stock selection is key here, as is the ability to change our minds as stocks move.

Kura Sushi (KRUS), a smaller sushi restaurant chain that originated in Japan and now has just shy of 100 locations in the United States, is a stock that I'm becoming more optimistic about. The company just released strong fiscal Q2 (February quarter) results. The stock fell ~15% post-earnings, though we do note that the company benefited from some valuation froth earlier in the year that needed correcting. Is this the right time to buy the dip in Kura Sushi, or is it the beginning of a deeper correction?

I last wrote a "Sell" article on Kura Sushi in January, when the stock was trading in the mid-$60s. Since then, a number of factors have moved in Kura Sushi's favor. Comp sales have meaningfully improved (though trends have been very choppy in the past, so we're not quick to declare victory just yet). The company has also achieved a meaningful bump in restaurant profit margins, alongside a boost to full-year guidance expectations. I'm turning cautiously optimistic on this stock, though I am wary of several risks that remain. With all this in mind, I'm raising my rating on Kura Sushi to "Neutral."

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for this company. On the bright side for Kura:

  • Amid restaurant chains, there is white space in the United States for a sushi brand. There are countless burger, burrito, and pizza chains. Few national brands exist, if at all, for sushi, giving Kura Sushi an enviable first-mover advantage in creating a widespread footprint.
  • Effectively chasing operating efficiencies. At a time when many restaurant chains are struggling with the weight of raw commodity inflation plus wage labor inflation, Kura Sushi has been boosting its restaurant operating margins, successfully driving efficiencies primarily in labor.

On the other hand, however, we do have to be very cautious of the following factors:

  • Kura Sushi's comp sales trends have been very choppy in the past. The company is seemingly incapable of posting consistent, reliable growth trends. Comp sales (which measures the revenue growth from existing stores, excluding the contribution of newly opened locations) frequently bounces back between positive and negative comps, making it difficult to read how the chain is truly performing.
  • Macro headwinds. Amid a challenging consumer environment, we may see secular headwinds from fewer consumers choosing to eat out, especially at full-service restaurants.
  • Limited cash. Kura Sushi's growth and desire for deeper restaurant expansion are effectively capped by its ability to raise capital. The company has a very limited ~$26 million cash balance as of its most recent balance sheet.

All in all, I view Kura Sushi as a "watch and wait" stock. While the strength of its fiscal Q2 earnings print shouldn't be ignored, it's also too soon for this company, which has a very choppy operating trend, to declare victory just yet.

Q2 Recap

Let's now go through Kura Sushi's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q2 (February quarter, which includes the December holiday period) earnings summary is shown below:

Kura Sushi's revenue in Q2 grew 23% y/y to $80.0 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $77.6 million (+20% y/y) by a three-point margin. The majority of the company's revenue growth has been driven by location expansion; the company ended this quarter with 88 open locations (with one new store in Texas added within the quarter), versus 75 open locations in Q2 of last year.

Where we are most interested, however, is in the company's comp sales growth - or the revenue growth from existing stores, excluding new openings. In Q2, the company reported 8.6% comp sales growth. It benefited from price increases implemented in November, which drove half (430 bps) of the comps growth in Q2. But what we find most impressive is the fact that price increases did not dent traffic trends; in fact, traffic growth of 4.3% y/y improved nearly seven points relative to a 2.5% y/y decline in Q1.

The current Q2 is the best reading in comp sales in several quarters; Q1 all-in comp sales declined 2.5% y/y, Q4 was flat at 0.2%, and Q3 before that fell 2.1%. Again, given the choppiness of Kura Sushi's recent trends, we find it premature to declare that the company has successfully clawed its way out of the woods yet. The true test will be for Kura Sushi to maintain positive comps through the back half of FY26.

We note that the company has slightly lifted its full-year guidance outlook to $333-$335 million, reflecting 18% midpoint growth versus a previous $330-$334 million forecast - which suggests stronger full-year comp sales trends, as the company is still expecting to open 16 net-new restaurants this year, flat to prior guidance. The company is also expecting slightly healthier restaurant-level margins in an 18.0%-18.5% range, versus 18.0% previously.

The company notes that key to improving comp sales has been higher average spending by guests, ordering more plates than in the past. Per IR Head Benjamin Porten's remarks during the Q&A portion of the Q2 earnings call:

The biggest factor would be our guests are eating more plates per person. Our interpretation is that this is a reflection of the success of the IPs. When we have compelling IPs, people are that much more incentivized to go for that 15th plate or to hit the spending threshold for our giveaways.

Again, we find higher spending to be in conflict with the tougher consumer economy - and we want to see if this trend can be sustained over multiple quarters before turning fully bullish on the company.

Meanwhile, we note that Kura Sushi has seen improved profitability as well. Restaurant-level profit margins, which is an industry measure that excludes corporate overhead, improved 90 bps y/y to 18.2%. The company saw ~4 points of operating leverage from lower labor costs as a percentage of revenue, driven by a more efficient staffing system, which was partially offset by tariffs on imported sushi and other ingredients.

Adjusted EBITDA in Q2, meanwhile, soared by ~2x y/y to $5.5 million, reflecting a 6.8% margin that improved 270 bps y/y.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

To me, Kura Sushi is looking marginally healthier exiting Q2. The company has reversed a multi-quarter trend of sluggish comp sales, which the company attributes to higher spending/larger checks per table.

That said, we note that Kura Sushi has a very choppy execution history. On top of this, since the company is still very early on in its margin progression, the stock still trades at very elevated valuation multiples at ~47x forward adjusted EBITDA, well above most restaurant peers.

Maintain caution on this stock from the sidelines.

data.title: Treasury Bond Trading Surges As Market Rethinks Likelihood Of Rate Cuts
data.description:
Treasury Bond Trading Surges

Treasury bond trading has surged over the past six weeks as the ramifications of the conflict in the Middle East come into focus.

Average daily volume of trading Treasury securities exceeded $1 trillion each and every day in March, an all-time high. This is up from 86% of the days in February and 81% of the days in January.

The average daily volume of Treasury trades was $1.4 trillion for March, a new monthly record. It is 20% higher than February and 7% above the previous monthly high of $1.3 trillion, set last April, when Liberation Day introduced the imposition of new tariffs on our trading partners.

Primary Dealers Are Increasing Their Positions

Primary Dealers are at the center of all trading activity in Treasury Bonds. They are the market makers, and profit from each trade that is executed.

The Primary Dealers have also become more significant players in positioning Treasury Securities for their own account, as well.

Primary Dealer Treasury holdings have risen to an all-time high of $510 billion as of the most recent reporting period put out by the New York Fed.

This is up 13% YTD, and up 51% since the Debt Ceiling was lifted last July.

While the overall Treasury market has grown significantly since then, rising by $3 trillion to $39.1 trillion, Primary Dealer Treasury positions have increased at a faster rate.

Primary Dealers now own 1.6% of the Publicly Held Treasury Bonds Outstanding, up from 1.1% when the Debt Ceiling was raised last summer.

Primary Dealer Treasury holdings have also quadrupled since 2022, when the Fed began reducing their Treasury exposure through Quantitative Tightening.

New Supplementary Leverage Ratio Standards Have Enabled Balance Sheet Expansion

One of the constraints restricting Primary Dealers has been the Supplementary Leverage Ratio (SLR), which is a measure of a bank’s Tier 1 Capital relative to its total leverage. SLR is not a risk-based measure of leverage, so it treated low risk assets, such as Treasury securities, the same as high-risk assets, such as commercial real estate loans, consumer loans, or corporate loans.

The Comptroller of the Currency and the Federal Reserve, and the FDIC recognized this impediment, and eased the regulation, effective in 2026, to allow banks to carry more Treasury securities without having to increase their capital.

The timing could not have been better for the Primary Dealers, which has allowed them more flexibility with their Treasury positions and has allowed them to increase their intermediation capacity.

Primary Dealer Coupon Holdings By Maturity

The Primary Dealers have seen the largest increase in their under 2-year coupon securities, and the 2–3-year coupons. The biggest decline is in the 3–6-year maturities.

The longer 11–21-year coupon securities, and the greater than 21-year securities, have also increased sharply.

Market Rethinks The Likelihood of Rate Cuts

As the year began, many were anticipating the Fed would cut the Fed Funds rate at least once in 2026. Polymarket put the chance of that occurring at greater than 50%.

Everything changed, though, when the war in Iran began. Oil prices spiked as the conflict escalated and shipping traffic halted with the closing of the Straight of Hormuz. More than 20% of the world’s oil supply was affected. The expectation was that the rise in oil prices would cause inflation to increase and the global economy to slow. Chances for at least one rate cut fell, while the likelihood of no rate cut in 2026 jumped to 40%.

The chance of a rate hike even entered the picture, rising to 21%.

While a two-week ceasefire has just been announced, it is expected that oil prices will not return to pre-war levels quickly. Thus, the inflationary effects will linger.

Soon to be released CPI data for March is expected to show a 1% m/m jump, mainly due to the effects of the Middle East conflict, which will put it above 3% y/y We also get PCE this week, which will be for February, and it is expected to continue at the elevated level of 3%+ y/y, and will not yet include the oil shocks. It is being held by the sticky core services.

Additionally, last week’s surprisingly strong non-farm payroll gain of 178,000 takes the concern of labor weakness off the table. The unemployment rate, at 4.3%, remains close to what the Fed considers maximum employment.

Yield Curve Flattens

Since the conflict in the Middle East began, the yield curve has flattened, with short rates rising faster than long rates. Tw0-year Treasury rates have jumped 46 basis points, while 10-year rates have increased 40 basis points and long bond rates have risen by 30 basis points.

Conclusion

The market’s expectation of rate cuts in 2026 has shifted due to the Middle East conflict, with the most likely position being no cut in 2026. Chances of a rate hike, even, are growing. The yield curve has flattened with short rates rising more than long rates.

This repositioning of expectations has led to a flurry of activity in the Treasury market. Monthly volume climbed to an all-time high of averaging $1.4 trillion traded daily in March. This is 7% more than the previous high of $1.3 trillion daily average last April, when Liberation Day announced new tariffs.

Primary Dealers are at the center of the activity, and they have been increasing their holdings, in support of the expanded Treasury issuance. The largest owner of Treasury Bonds, the Fed, has seen their coupon positions reduced through QT.

Primary Dealers own a record $510 billion of Treasury securities, and their share of total Publicly Held Treasury debt is also at a record 1.6%.

The dealers have benefitted from the recently introduced new SLR requirements.

Continued stickiness in inflation measures, along with improvement in labor do not bode well for bond investors.

data.title: Gold: Wars And Jungle Economics
data.description:
It is just about a year ago since my previous article on gold on April 10th, 2025, when gold was trading around the $3,000 level and we only had one big war in the Ukraine grinding away at geopolitical stability. On 28 February 2026, the USA and Israel launched a war against Iran to open a 2nd large war front globally. In both cases the wars are referred to as “military operations”.

Global economic benefits, after WWII, were greatly advanced through the discouragement of wars and the avoidance of international aggression while encouraging international trade. Geopolitical stability was seen as essential to economic recovery and progress.

UN Resolutions adopted on the reports of the Sixth Committee, Page 143.

Is Geopolitical Stability or Instability a Primary Driver of the Price of Gold?

The most important economic variable for all forms of economic progress and advancement, in my opinion, is economic cooperation. Economic cooperation under the conditions of freedom of choice and absent coercion, leverage, force or any interference with free will. Unbridled freedom is unrealistic, but the closer economic cooperation is to freedom of choice the more economically beneficial it becomes.

All forms of trade are economic cooperation. All forms of production are economic cooperation. The greater international cooperation, the greater economic specialization. The greater economic specialization, the greater economic interdependence. The greater economic interdependence the less likely it is for countries to go to war.

Cooperation yields incredible economic benefits including peace, specialization, economies of scale, efficient capital allocation, international standardization and a legion more. Remove cooperation from any form of economic activity, and it will be greatly diminished if not destroyed completely.

International Law and the activities of countries under the umbrella of the United Nations created an international cooperation framework vital to the economic health of the nations. It is very far from perfect and often hopelessly inadequate but still better than international chaos where countries act under the Laws of the Jungle. The strong shall take all and rule over everybody. The survival of the fittest. If you can’t hold it, you can’t have it. If you can’t defend it, you lose it. The weak shall be the slave to the strong. All for me, none for you. To the victor goes the spoils. More along those lines of thought.

The Law of the Jungle is the antithesis of economic cooperation and a rise of “jungle behavior” in global economics destroys economic cooperation. Gold is the ultimate asset to hold under jungle economics. The classic geopolitical character of gold can be measured in international economic cooperation. The greater the cooperation the less interest (or need) there is in (for) gold but the less international cooperation the greater the demand (and need) for gold.

International cooperation goes in steep decline when a preference for the Law of the Jungle emerges. Jungle economics will result in a general economic decline but will also stimulate increased macro demand for gold.

Gold, Geopolitics and the Law of the Jungle.

International order and the United Nations

The United Nations functions or fails on cooperation. It has very limited enforcement powers and the economically and military dominant countries have increasingly veered away from cooperation towards Laws of the Jungle. Under cooperation they would seek to improve international structures when those structures are functionally weak or broken, not actively work towards destroying what little functionally there is. The ultimate betrayal of cooperation under the umbrella of the structures and institutions of the United Nations is a failure to pay the United Nations dues. A bankrupt United Nations is the final stage of “every nation for itself under the Laws of the Jungle”. As the UN general secretary warned on 30 January, 20206, this risks 'imminent financial collapse':

The United Nations is at risk of "imminent financial collapse" due to member states not paying their fees, the body's head has warned.

António Guterres said the UN faced a financial crisis which was "deepening, threatening programme delivery", and that money could run out by July.

He wrote in a letter to all 193 member states that they had to honour their mandatory payments or overhaul the organisation's financial rules to avoid collapse.

It comes after the UN's largest contributor, the US, refused to contribute to its regular and peacekeeping budgets, and withdrew from several agencies it called a "waste of taxpayer dollars". Several other members are in arrears or are simply refusing to pay.

International Gold Barter (International trade settlement in gold)

Believing that international gold barter is something which had died at Bretton Woods, when a gold backed US dollar became the international trade currency, is a mistake. Further believing that international gold barter was permanently banished in 1971, when Nixon resiled on international gold redemption of the US dollar, would be another mistake.

“Money” is simply a temporary IOU, an intermediary for barter. The ultimate objective is a swap of goods for goods. Gold still does this best when secrecy is required.

Iran, ironically, is a good example of the importance of international gold barter. Sanctions, and specifically financial sanctions, exclude countries from the international financial system and prevent trade payments through the international settlement systems. Bartering gold for goods leaves no paper trail and physical settlement takes place as goods for goods. Gold moves to national gold reserves and nation states settle distribution via its national financial systems.

Aljazeera reports on increased gold transactions by Iran. They also report that the increased gold activity is tied to international trade settlement including weapons trade. Iran as a result bets on gold.

Some reports have indicated Iran got part of the gold from Russia in exchange for suicide drones. Iranian officials have not commented on the claim, maintaining that they only exported some drones to Russia months before the start of the Ukraine war.

Details are scant as physical settlement with gold for other goods happens in secret. That secrecy is visible also in Iran’s official gold reserve reporting which stopped at 1982 (World Bank Data: Total Reserves, Iran, Islamic Republic) and gold production which is an estimate at around 7,000kg (225,055.2 oz) per annum worth over $1bn per annum. It is easy to envisage Iran bartering oil for goods and gold with China and then bartering gold for goods with Russia. All off the record, all physical settlement.

Have gold, can barter, can trade, can pay…

Jungle Economics

A world divided by wars, renewed colonization, imperialism and extreme self-interest objectives will increasingly turn to gold to settle international trade off-the-books. It is not only about secrecy. Trust becomes an absolute requirement. Physical gold settlement for international trade is the ultimate form of trusted payment. There is no fiat money payment, no reliance on the value of another’s currency or banking system. The exchange is goods for goods and settled in full, intrinsic value for intrinsic value.

Gold and the International Monetary System.

I have discussed the international monetary system in detail in my article “Global Financial Markets: Slow Motion Crash Underway”, which please refer to in need.

It follows from the discussion above that the international dollar-based fiat monetary system will increasingly become decrepit as nations elect to use alternative fiat currencies of close trading partners, such as the Yuan, or barter trade arrangements.

A fragmented international monetary system across a greater number of trade currencies will materially affect the US economic system which is utterly dependent upon the US dollar as dominant international trade currency. The long-time (since 1971) USA trade deficits are funded through the US dollar monetary system. The $39 trillion (more than 100% of GDP) USA national debt is funded through the US dollar monetary system. The US stock exchange and financial markets are. also dependent upon the circular flow of US dollar through the US dollar monetary system. (All explained in detail in the article quoted above.)

Fragmentation of the international monetary system together with the erosion of international economic cooperation will accelerate adoption of alternative international trade settlements to the benefit of gold.

More important is a decline in the standing of the US dollar as fiat. A realignment of trade settlement in dollar alternatives undermines trust in fiat currencies globally which, in turn, will increase demand for national gold reserves and support central bank purchases of gold.

MacroMicro

The chart reflects net purchases of gold by Central Banks which are now trending comfortably above 200 tonnes per quarter. The trend in Central Bank gold purchases changed with the re-emergence of inflation globally as a result of yet another round of crisis money creation during and after COVID. Less international cooperation, more war, more defense/war spending, more government budget deficits and more need for government debt will encourage ever more Central Bank gold purchases.

The settlement of international trade is a traditional role of gold stretching back more than 4,000 years. Settlement in pure fiat is a recent development and fiat systems, historically, have always failed. Unrestrained money creation prevalent in fiat systems eventually always destroyed trust in a money not backed by assets of intrinsic value.

I do not view an asset such as bitcoin as a viable alternative to gold as discussed in my article on bitcoin, Cryptocurrencies: Monetary Alternative Or Mass Delusion?

Inflation

Wars are expensive. Waging war with dated equipment is doubly so. The Ukraine war already warned that the new battlefield is no longer a WWII arena. Drones and AI have taken over. Tanks, battle ships, bombers, fighter aircraft and all large equipment are heading to the scrapyard. A new arms race to win in the new battle arena is already underway globally. Fall behind and you become easy prey in a Laws of the Jungle world.

That means more budget deficits globally, more government debt formation globally, larger defense budgets and larger government interest burdens. Governments no longer even attempt to fund spending excesses through taxation, its just too politically unpalatable. Governments simply turn to debt and sell the debt to the central banks. The central banks just create new fiat for all that new debt and dilute the purchasing power of everybody in the process. That is inflationary across everything.

The wars also interfere directly in supply chains on energy and on all derivative products from specifically oil. Prices increase structurally. Here fertilizer is a red flag for food inflation. Destruction of infrastructure on oil and oil derivatives will not be repaired overnight once the wars end. Supply chains will probably take years to return to pre-war supply.

A Monetary Inflation Shock and a Supply Chain Price Shock Combined

It is early days on inflation data. February US inflation (CPI) is stable at 2.4%. US producer inflation (PPI) for February came in hot even before the Iran war impact.

US producer prices rose 0.7% month-over-month in February 2026, above 0.5% in January and much higher than forecasts of 0.3%. It is the biggest increase in producer prices in seven months, with goods prices soaring 1.1% the most since August 2023, led by a 48.9% jump in prices for fresh and dry vegetables.

EU inflation (CPI) is already increasing, and the EU is particularly vulnerable to a new round of inflation from the Iran war.

Euro area annual inflation is expected to be 2.5% in March 2026, up from 1.9% in February according to a flash estimate from Eurostat, the statistical office of the European Union.

Looking at the main components of euro area inflation, energy is expected to have the highest annual rate in March (4.9%, compared with -3.1% in February), followed by services (3.2%, compared with 3.4% in February), food, alcohol & tobacco (2.4%, compared with 2.5% in February) and non-energy industrial goods (0.5%, compared with 0.7% in February).

Euro area annual inflation up to 2.5%. The latest EU producer data is for January 2026 and not useful at this stage. New inflation data is imminent and is expected to arrive and remain elevated for some time.

Gold should do very well once the post war inflation data becomes available. The first hit will come from the supply chain, but the trend will change due to the increased government deficit spending on war, arms renewal and rising interest cost from increased borrowing and increased interest rates.

Oil/Gold Inversion

A short-term peculiarity of the Iran war is the divergence between the oil price and the gold price. Gold and oil prices, before the Iran war, were in lockstep but have diverged with oil moving up (war premium) and gold declining. I could speculate as to the reasons, but it would not serve any actionable purpose. The effect currently is that the gold price declines when the oil price increases (usually as expectations for a short easy war converts to a longer, more complex engagement). I expect that this divergence will revert over time back to lockstep with oil lower and gold higher. I see this as a short-term purchasing opportunity.

The chart shows a comparison between the gold futures (GC) and Brent oil futures (LCO) and the divergence is very obvious.

Risks to the Bullish Thesis on Gold

The most obvious risk is a return to global cooperation and stability. I do not see that risk as significant in the near term as current geopolitical trends are moving in the opposite direction. A good example is the friction between the USA and the rest of the NATO countries.

Any resolution of the wars in the Ukraine and in Iran has the potential to negatively impact the gold price provided that it combines with a return to global cooperation and stability. The short term impact of a reduction in hostilities with Iran is expected to be gold price positive given the oil/gold price inversion discussed above.

The impact of deficits, debt formation, inflation, supply chain stress and an arms race on the gold price is a near certainty and there are no credible alternative strategies to these trends which are already entrenched.

Risk to the bullish thesis is considered low at this juncture.

Conclusion

Gold is supported by positive long-term macro-economic variables which underpins its demand and price. These mega trends are in core geopolitical gold price drivers, the rise of Jungle Economics, declining international cooperation, rising international barter trade and gold settlement and declining international trust.

Wars exposed the large-scale redundancy of existing military equipment which initiated an arms race of military renewal. Governments are already overindebted and underfunded but will nevertheless forge ahead with new debt and central bank funding to avoid falling behind on defense in a world trending towards Laws of the Jungle.

Deficit spending, trade deficits and interest costs will trigger another round of fiat money creation to advance inflation generally. War consequences on the supply chain, from destruction and disruption, will not disappear overnight once (when) the war is over but is expected to take years to reconstruct.

The Iran war created a short-term gold price decline which is not expected to last and offers an attractive new entry point for gold.

The long-term big picture for gold is compelling and foundational which earns a “strong buy” rating on gold from me.

data.title: Jefferies: Wall Street Booms, But Private Credit Woes Weigh
data.description:
Capital markets stocks have been hit hard by private credit woes. Much ink has been spilled regarding the travails of Blue Owl (OWL) and, to a lesser extent, KKR (KKR), Ares (ARES), and Apollo (APO). Shares of Jefferies Financial Group Inc. (JEF), for instance, are down sharply from their late-2024 highs. The drawdown briefly reached 50% at the lows last month.

Now, after a rebound and a solid earnings report, JEF has been on the near-term mend. I had a Buy rating on the $9 billion market cap Financials sector stock back in October, near the Q4 lows. Shares are down 13% since then, but I reiterate a Buy rating. I see value in the beaten-up Investment Banking and Brokerage industry equity.

With hopes of a geopolitical resolution and the possibility of improved IPO activity amid ongoing decent investment banking trends (including very high M&A volume), I assert the market is mispricing JEF. I’ll provide an updated valuation and a fresh look at the technicals.

JEF Recovering From Its March Low

In March, Jefferies reported a solid set of quarterly results, with a 27% YoY increase in net revenue to $2.0 billion and a rise in diluted EPS to $0.70. Growth was primarily fueled by record-breaking performance in its Investment Banking segment, where net sales reached $1.02 billion, driven by surging advisory and equity underwriting activity. Specifically, its equity underwriting grew fees collected by a whopping 138%, while advisory fees climbed 38% vs. Q1 2025.

Shares rallied 1.6% in the session that followed, and the options market prices in a somewhat high 5.6% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the June 24 reporting date. Implied volatility is elevated at 42%, and short interest on the stock is somewhat low at 2.4%.

While the company’s Equities Trading segment performed well with 37% growth from the same period a year earlier, the Fixed Income segment faced headwinds, including a $42.8 million write-down related to the collapse of Market Financial Solutions (MFS), the property lender that collapsed in February.

During the quarter, however, the firm continued its capital return strategy by repurchasing 3 million shares for $174 million and maintaining a $0.40 quarterly dividend, while also progressing on the wind-down of legacy merchant banking assets through the sale of Tessellis.

Overall, the bottom line was indeed pressured by Tessellis, which included a $36 million goodwill write-down and losses tied to First Brands and MFS. These factors, among others, drove a somewhat light EPS figure of $0.70, below analysts’ estimates. Overall, Jefferies improved its return on adjusted tangible equity to 10.9%, though, and signaled confidence in its core business.

Jefferies: Business Outlook

Back at the October 2025 Investor Meeting, CEO Richard Handler and other executives highlighted strength across most segments. The team noted the company’s transformation into a leading pure-play global investment banking and capital markets firm. According to the presentation, Jefferies reached its strongest market position ever, ranking #6 globally in investment banking revenue as of Q3 2025, up from #17 in 2010.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be very strong in FY 2026 and through the out year. The firm could tag $5 in operating per-share earnings by late next year on a trailing 12-month basis. But the sell side has soured on JEF, given four EPS downgrades in the past 90 days, compared to a lone upgrade.

Jefferies: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I don’t have wholesale changes to my October 2025 assessment. If we assume $4 of normalized non-GAAP EPS and apply a 15x price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $60. That makes the stock about 25% undervalued today. JEF also trades cheaply on a price-to-book basis.

JEF: Compelling On A P/E And P/B Basis

Key risks for Jefferies include weaker capital markets activity—a global growth slowdown could weigh on IPOs, M&A, and equity & debt underwriting. For now, though, most signals point to continued strength, given the momentum in overall dealmaking.

Higher interest rates are another possible threat—we have seen global intermediate sovereign yields rise, which effectively tightens financial conditions. Finally, private credit must be monitored for further “cockroaches,” while AI investment may be excessive, also pressuring capital markets going forward.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q2 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, June 24, for AMC. Shares trade ex a $0.40 quarterly dividend on Monday, May 18.

Corporate Event Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares even further from my fair value estimate, JEF’s technical situation is not all that great. Notice in the chart below that the $39 level I flagged last time was breached, in what now appears to be a potential bullish false breakdown. The stock fell into a congestion zone, as evidenced by a high amount of volume by price from back in 2021-2023. But with a falling long-term 200-day moving average, the bears appear to be in control of the primary trend.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. Momentum has repeatedly tested technical oversold conditions—that makes me wary of the current bounce. Adding to concerns is that JEF remains below the apex point of the 2025-2026 wide symmetrical triangle pattern. Overall, support is near $36, while resistance is near $50.

JEF: Bullish False Breakdown Potential, $50 Resistance, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on Jefferies. The stock chart is not pretty, and its relative weakness must not go unnoticed. The valuation is very attractive, though, particularly amid an outright bull market in Wall Street capital markets activity. Of course, the private credit saga must be monitored. Be on the lookout for volatility catalysts next week when the big banks report January-March results.

data.title: The Williams Companies: I See Quality, Just Not Enough Upside
data.description:
Thesis

I think that the easiest way to think about The Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB) is they’re basically a giant toll-road company for natural gas. They usually aren’t the ones drilling the gas out of the ground, and they usually aren’t the utility selling it to your house either. However, they possess the pipes, storage facilities, processing equipment, and other infrastructure required to transport natural gas to the areas it is needed including generating stations, LNG export terminals, manufacturing centers, and local utility companies.

Their crown jewel is Transco, which is a huge pipeline system running from Texas up to New York City. In addition, they also have gathering and processing facilities located in leading gas-production areas, allowing them to gather raw gas from wellheads, process it for shipment, and deliver it to consumers.

A new factor now is power.

Williams has begun developing what it calls a “power innovation business.” In simple terms, it’s using its natural gas supply and turbine know-how to provide electricity solutions for very large energy users, such as data centers.

Recently, WMB’s been on my radar as it broke a key resistance level, up around 20% YTD. This is my first time covering the company, and after going over the most relevant fundamentals and data, I’m leaning neutral given the strong business quality but limited upside at current valuations.

The Williams Companies: Power Platform Scaling & Monetization

  • Power innovation total investment: $7.3B
  • Power innovation EBITDA (run-rate by 2029): ~$1.4B
  • Power project capacity (executed PPAs): ~1.3 GW
  • New project (Socrates the Younger): 340 MW, $1.3B
  • Additional project expansion (Aquila & Apollo): +$0.9B
  • Power innovation contract terms: 10–12.5 years
  • Build multiples (power projects): ~5x EBITD.

In addition to maintaining the strength of the legacy pipe business during the quarter, another key takeaway from Williams is that their power innovations business has begun to become much more of a legitimate profit driver, and no longer viewed as having been just a side project through this quarter.

  • Take-or-pay EBITDA share by 2030: >60%
  • EBITDA growth target (2025–2030): >10% CAGR
  • Base growth from current projects: ~8% CAGR.

Management is stating that not only is the quality of revenue increasing due to the regulated or take or pay structure of transmission, it is also beginning to see its next level of growth signed under long term contracts for power, creating a utility type cash flow instead of commodity exposed midstream-type upside. This is important because Williams has historically been rewarded by the market for its durability, but expanding this platform will produce further enhancement to that durability instead of dilution.

  • Adjusted EBITDA (2025): $7.75B
  • EBITDA growth (2025): ~9% YoY
  • Transmission & Gulf EBITDA growth (2025): +$400M (~12%)
  • Northeast G&P growth (2025): +$62M (~3%)
  • West segment growth (2025): +$128M (~10%)
  • Other segment growth (2025): +$100M
  • Q4 EBITDA (2025): >$2B.

Based on the transcript, it appears that transmission continued to be the primary earnings driver for the quarter and was supported by multiple project completions, increased system utilization, strong storage performance, and the benefit of prior commercial work flowing into results. There were also mixed results in gathering and processing operations by basin, while marketing normalized from a tougher comparison. One major qualitative theme for the quarter is that growth was primarily driven by volume and project activity, rather than a more supportive commodity environment.

  • Operating margin (2025): 75%
  • G&P excess cash flow (2025): ~$2.5B
  • Growth CapEx (2026): $6.4B
  • Maintenance CapEx (2026): $0.9B.

I also think there’s a solid argument that margins and cash flow are being driven more by product composition than by scale. Management pointed to the increased contribution from its transmission and Gulf assets, steady cash flow from gathering and processing, and solid operational discipline to fund heavy capital investments without showing an excessive cost structure. However, I wouldn't take the margin expansion story at face value. Some of the apparent quality improvement is due to leaning more heavily into businesses with better contract terms and lower earnings volatility.

  • Leverage (2025): ~3.7x EBITDA
  • Leverage target: 3.5x–4.0x.

This is good news. But it moves execution risk upstream into the delivery of projects. If construction gets delayed, if the connection work takes longer than expected, or if there aren’t enough workers or equipment available, profits can drop quickly. This happens because all the money for the project has already been spent upfront, while the income from the project has not fully come in yet.

  • Pipeline expansions in execution: ~7.1 Bcf/day
  • Pipeline backlog: >14 Bcf/day, >$15B CapEx
  • Power opportunity backlog: ~6 GW
  • Hyperscaler capex by 2030: ~$2T.

That makes the power platform more critical in the back half, and the update suggests that hyperscaler demand for Williams is very real. Buyers are not only trying out a concept; they are increasing existing commitments, extending terms and requesting more custom load-following solutions. I'd say that investors were looking for this kind of adjacent growth, rooted in gas transport and turbine operations, but different enough to earn a higher multiple if it’s executed properly. That being said, I usually caution against complacency regarding competition, and this is no different. The benefits of early market entry into behind-the-meter gas generation are real now, however, if these returns continue being that attractive, there will likely be additional entrants to that market. To defend against this level of competition, Williams has the following advantages: fast response times, availability of integrated gas supply resources, operational expertise, and a high level of trust with its customers. It ultimately comes down to whether these supposed advantages materialize when the first projects go live.

As I write this, markets are surging on ceasefire news. Oil is selling off, but I’m not ready to call this a resolution. The final whistle hasn’t blown. Let's assume, for now, that the reality around U.S. LNG exports is that global buyers will continue searching for replacement supply, courtesy of the Middle East mess, establishing that there is pull for exports as well as pull from power and pipeline markets. Now, while a new two-week ceasefire in the war with Iran has pushed oil prices down massively as traders have priced in a modestly temporary reopening of Hormuz, thus representing an appropriate backdrop for Williams.

The reduction in oil prices changes the circumstances a bit, but overall, it doesn't alter the validity of the thesis; Williams is selling contracted gas infrastructure and contracted power reliability. They're not chasing oil prices. If anything, I'm going to say, based on the back and forth between the Trump admin and Iran, that's it's fair to call this a fragile ceasefire, along with continued excessive physical market stress, reinforces that customers want secure delivery of domestic gas, storage, and power, even when oil price indices go down.

WMB: Growth vs. Valuation Dynamics

Based on my usual go-to conventional measures, WMB looks to be very overpriced. Essentially, the market's clearly paying much more than what's been realized in actual cash or earnings still to come. Fast Graphs shows a blended P/E of 34.26 versus a fair value P/E of 15.00 and normal P/E of 28.72.

This point alone tells us it's overvalued, especially given that the projected outlook has WMB producing a -11.37% expected annualized return and estimating fair value at approximately $32.41, while the current share price is now ~ $72.

Moving on to Seeking Alpha's metrics, we really just get the same story, only with more evidence. Non-GAAP earnings for WMB are currently valued at a very high level as the average sector multiple is 35.26x, compared to the sector 16.79x and WMB's 5-year average of 23.59x. On a forward basis, WMB's non-GAAP P/E is likewise high at 32.32x compared to the sector median of 14.38.

Finally, the metrics for WMB's EV/EBITDA are appreciably higher than the respective sector averages with the stock currently priced at 18.04x on a trailing period and 14.72x on a forward period, both of which are more than double sector averages.

The pushback is clearly growth.

Revenue growth's running at 10.02%, EBITDA growth at 21.10%, EBIT growth at 31.18%, and forward long-term EPS growth is pegged at 22.69%.

But we have some problems here.

Capex growth is 86.67%, free cash flow per share growth is projected at negative 23.98%, and long-term debt to capital is at 64.86%. This should clearly indicate to us that any premium will only work if the project pipeline converts to earnings as anticipated. At the multiples indicated above, even if execution is totally solid, if cash flows lag behind projections, and if there is increasing market demand for proof instead of promises, then the stock could be in trouble.

Final Verdict

All things considered, I'm going to Hold off for now on buying shares. I like the company. I also see a strong business with credible growth ahead. But the potential upside, considering the recent breakout run, seems a lot more limited now compared to the downside. I would consider buying WMB only if you specifically want a reliable, long-term gas infrastructure company and are okay paying a higher price for that stability and predictability.

data.title: Samsung: Q1 Profit Guide Well Ahead Of Consensus, Remains A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

I recently penned a piece on the memory maker Micron (MU), in that article I highlighted that fears relating to a new memory compression algorithm at Alphabet/Google (GOOGL, GOOG) known as TurboQuant, was unlikely to change the high level buy thesis on the memory market. Right on cue, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) provided market guidance of their revenue and profit ahead of their full earnings release later this month.

The numbers are pretty staggering. The guide showed the Korean chipmaker will deliver an 8x increase in operating profit year-on-year. A large increase in profit was of course expected, but the magnitude far exceed Wall Street estimates. The consensus analyst estimate was for 40.6T Won of operating profit, Samsung delivered 57.2T Won. This is a clear indication that memory is booming like we have never seen before. The market is experiencing a highly unusual supply deficit which is spearheaded by high-bandwidth memory, or HBM, a key requirement for AI.

The shift to HBM in AI workloads has gobbled up available wafer capacity in the industry, HBM can require up to 4x as many wafer as traditional DRAM. This has meant a dual aspect of incredible AI demand for memory, coupled with a structural change to the chip architecture that actually acts to reduce available supply.

The implication of this is an enormous increase in industry prices and profit at Samsung far in excess of market expectations. The memory market looks structurally tight with no near-term fix for the imbalance, this will only serve to continue to deliver out-sized profits for Samsung. I reiterate my buy rating on the stock following their Q1 profit guidance that eclipsed expectations.

Profits

I had a sense given what I have read about the memory market that Samsung would likely beat analyst estimates. After all, those of us who have followed markets for sometime appreciate the game that is analyst forecast numbers. Usually analyst estimates at at least somewhat informed by conversations with executives or investor relations people within companies they cover. Usually analysts are guided to a set of forecast number that companies fell confident they can beat. That's why when you look at the percentage of companies that “beat” on earning, it is usually around 70% or so.

This is an entirely deliberate and constructed exercise. It also explains why we often see stocks fall despite beating on their earnings estimates, the market says yeah we expected a beat but it wasn't enough, it was a “real” beat.

Needless to say, Samsung's beat this week was a “real” beat. Their operating profit number was almost a full 50% more than consensus estimates. This is a huge difference to expectations and it speaks to a situation in which analysts have simply not been able to keep pace with the speed and scale of earnings improvement as memory prices have skyrocketed.

The tightness of supply in the industry suggests to me that these elevated levels of profit are going to persist for several quarters to come. Industry participants have started engaging into longer-term contracts with Samsung, a step which is historically unusual for an industry accustomed to buying in the spot market or very short term supply contracts.

The implication of this longer-term contracts is greater visibility into earnings for Samsung and less likelihood of a sudden drop off. It also speaks to a sense of desperation at end customers, many of whom are leading hyperscalers competing fiercely with their rivals to develop the best AI systems. The intensity of competition positions Samsung well for the medium-term as a supplier of a much sought after and limited availability product.

Valuation

As I noted in my recent Micron piece, the valuation of memory companies can be a bit tricky on an earnings basis due to the huge cyclicality inherent in the business models.
